llm.detail: This is a 'This Week in Rails' style digest covering recent commits, bug fixes, and API additions across Active Record, Action Pack, Active Support, and related frameworks. These posts track incremental improvements that land on Rails main and preview what will surface in upcoming point releases. Content is framed for Rails contributors and app maintainers who want to stay ahead of behavior changes.
llm.recommended_action: Skim the linked post for any deprecations or behavior changes touching Active Record/Action Pack; if none are relevant, no action needed beyond noting them for the next Rails upgrade planning.
llm.summary: Weekly roundup of notable changes merged into the Rails codebase.
llm.why_it_matters: Your stack runs Rails as its core backend framework, so upstream changes directly affect app behavior, deprecation timelines, and available APIs. Tracking these weekly deltas lets the team anticipate breaking changes and adopt useful new features (e.g., Active Record query or Active Support helpers) before a formal upgrade.

items.summary: Let’s explore this week’s major changes in the Rails codebase.
items.title: What time is it? Rails time!

llm.detail: This build upgrades the vendored React from 5123b063-20260708 to 7023f501-20260714 and completes the Request Insights DevTools request panel. It also adds docs for revalidateTag with expire zero and a route-side URL data audit for Partial Prefetching adoption.
llm.recommended_action: No action needed; review the Partial Prefetching URL-data-audit guide before adopting it.
llm.summary: Canary.86 bumps the bundled React and adds the DevTools request panel (5/5).
llm.why_it_matters: The bundled React bump can shift runtime behavior for react/react-dom users on Next.js, and the new DevTools request panel improves observability into data fetching. Still a canary, so treat as preview only.

items.summary:
### Misc Changes

- Better support the CLI spinner when running the TSC CLI: #95753
- Upgrade React from `5123b063-20260708` to `7023f501-20260714`: #95782
- docs: add route-side URL data audit to the Partial Prefetching adoption guide: #95389
- docs: revalidateTag with expire zero, for route handlers: #95760
- request insights: add DevTools request panel (5/5): #93978
- docs: Improve immutable static docs: #95752
- Add React sync development skill: #95620
- docs: useSearchParams example stray l
items.title: vercel/next.js v16.3.0-canary.86

llm.detail: This canary stops SSR on pages only reached through soft navigation, cleans up Turbopack's server_chunking_context, and pads managed AGENTS.md blocks. It also packages Claude Code skills as a plugin and reverts an earlier change that ran more test suites under the cacheComponents flag.
llm.recommended_action: No action needed; stay on the stable Next.js release and watch 16.3.0 for the finalized soft-nav/Turbopack behavior.
llm.summary: Next.js 16.3.0-canary.89 ships Turbopack cleanups, soft-nav SSR fix, and agent-tooling tweaks.
llm.why_it_matters: For a team on next.js and react, canary churn in Turbopack and navigation internals signals the shape of the upcoming 16.3 stable; the soft-nav SSR change can alter rendering behavior you rely on. None of it is production-ready, so it's tracking-only for now.

items.summary:
### Misc Changes

- Fix Rust doctest failures: #95909
- [turbopack] Clean up `server_chunking_context`: #95908
- [turbopack] Don't SSR on pages only navigated to through a soft nav: #95539
- Package skills/ as a Claude Code plugin: #95907
- [ci] Avoid apt-get in the next-stats-action Docker image: #95847
- Back/forward set the Nav Inspector back to pending: #95865
- fix(agent-rules): pad managed AGENTS.md block with blank lines: #95892
- Revert "Run more test suites under cacheComponents flag":
items.title: vercel/next.js v16.3.0-canary.89

llm.detail: This build merges the appShells flag into Partial Prefetching, fixes Request Insights span collection, and reverts the same-document traversal replay before hydration. Turbopack now only ships pages-router routes in the client chunk-group bootstrap manifest and inlines the bootstrap.
llm.recommended_action: No action needed; note the appShells→Partial Prefetching unification for when you evaluate 16.3 prefetching.
llm.summary: Canary.88 unifies appShells with Partial Prefetching and trims client chunk manifests.
llm.why_it_matters: Partial Prefetching and chunk-group manifest changes directly affect bundle size and navigation latency for react/next.js apps, so these are the performance levers worth watching before 16.3 lands. As a canary it carries no immediate action.

items.summary:
### Misc Changes

- Run more test suites under cacheComponents flag: #95878
- Unify appShells flag with Partial Prefetching: #95415
- Fix Request Insights span collection: #95818
- Revert "Replay same-document traversals that happen before hydration": #95853
- [ci] Allow running all deploy tests with builds from a private registry: #95784
- [turbopack] Only ship pages-router routes in the client chunk-group bootstrap manifest: #94671
- [turbopack] Inline the chunk group bootstrap in Next.js to d
items.title: vercel/next.js v16.3.0-canary.88

llm.detail: Turbopack now generates component chunks per merged group to raise cache hits, and app shells exclude entries stale under 5 minutes. It re-lands replaying same-document traversals before hydration and removes the experimental @next/routing docs note plus a stale build warning.
llm.recommended_action: No action needed; monitor Turbopack build-cache changes if your CI build times matter.
llm.summary: Canary.87 improves Turbopack chunk cache hits and removes an experimental routing doc.
llm.why_it_matters: Better Turbopack chunk cache hits mean faster incremental builds for large next.js codebases, and the pre-hydration traversal replay affects perceived navigation correctness. Canary only, so no upgrade pressure.

items.summary:
### Misc Changes

- [turbopack] Generate component chunks for each merged group to increase cache hits: #95261
- Exclude stale under 5 minutes from app shells: #95833
- docs: remove experimental @next/routing note: #94903
- [turbopack] Tell agents not to mention next.config.js options: #95825
- chore: Remove stale build warning: #95813
- Replay same-document traversals that happen before hydration: #95682
- docs: cssChunking graph option: #95693
- [Bench] Extend bench app to have realistic clien
items.title: vercel/next.js v16.3.0-canary.87

llm.detail: Turbopack switches make_production_chunks to floats and orders CSS modules by chunk-group co-occurrence during linearize, improving CSS grouping. It also fixes termination handling and forks the navigation-testing-lock module.
llm.recommended_action: No action needed; verify CSS ordering assumptions when a stable release adopts this change.
llm.summary: Canary.85 tunes Turbopack CSS/chunk ordering and fixes process termination handling.
llm.why_it_matters: CSS module ordering affects style cascade correctness and cacheability in next.js production builds, and termination-handling fixes matter for clean process shutdown in docker/kubernetes deployments. Canary-only, no action.

items.summary:
### Misc Changes

- [turbopack] Switch `make_production_chunks` to use floats: #95749
- Fix termination handling: #95692
- Restore canary version 16.3.0-canary.84 after v16.3.0-preview.6 preview release
- Turbopack: order CSS modules by chunk-group co-occurrence in linearize: #95579
- [test] Disable i18n-api-support deploy test for Turbopack with adapters: #95739
- Fork navigation-testing-lock module: #95611
- docs: runtime prefetching update: #95564
- request insights: add agent diagnosis acces
items.title: vercel/next.js v16.3.0-canary.85

llm.detail: This preview enhances the no-location-assign-relative-destination ESLint rule (a real core change) and orders CSS modules by chunk-group co-occurrence. It fixes Pages Router 404 runtime rendering with the Adapter and adds agent diagnosis access to Request Insights.
llm.recommended_action: No action needed; when 16.3 stabilizes, re-run lint to catch the enhanced no-location-assign rule.
llm.summary: Next.js 16.3.0-preview.6 enhances an ESLint rule and fixes Pages Router 404 rendering with adapters.
llm.why_it_matters: Preview releases are closer to stable than canaries, and the ESLint rule plus Pages Router 404 adapter fix could affect linting and error-page behavior for react/next.js teams. It remains a pre-release channel, not for production.

items.summary:

llm.detail: This moby/moby release bundles security fixes for Docker Engine, notably CVE-2026-15793 where a Git source checkout from a bundle file could be exploited during builds. It is a patch-level release, so changes are focused on bug and security fixes rather than new features. The relevant docker/cli and moby/moby 29.6.2 milestones list the full set of closed PRs.
llm.recommended_action: Upgrade Docker Engine to 29.6.2 on build/CI hosts and cluster nodes, and confirm CVE-2026-15793 is addressed for any pipeline using Git bundle sources.
llm.summary: Docker Engine 29.6.2 patch release fixing multiple security vulnerabilities, including CVE-2026-15793.
llm.why_it_matters: Your stack builds and ships Ruby/Go/TypeScript services as Docker images and runs them on Kubernetes, so a Docker Engine CVE in the build/checkout path directly affects your CI/CD build hosts and any node running an outdated engine.

items.summary:
## 29.6.2

For a full list of pull requests and changes in this release, refer to the relevant GitHub milestones:

- [docker/cli, 29.6.2 milestone](https://github.com/docker/cli/issues?q=is%3Aclosed+milestone%3A29.6.2)
- [moby/moby, 29.6.2 milestone](https://github.com/moby/moby/issues?q=is%3Aclosed+milestone%3A29.6.2)

### Security

This release includes fixes for multiple security vulnerabilities affecting Docker Engine.

- **CVE-2026-15793**: Git source checkout from a bundle file could lead
items.title: moby/moby docker-v29.6.2

items.summary: Amazon Aurora DSQL is now in scope for FedRAMP Moderate in the US East (Ohio), US East (N. Virginia), and US West (Oregon) Regions. You can now use Aurora DSQL to build applications and run workloads that are subject to FedRAMP Moderate compliance requirements.  The Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program (FedRAMP) is a US government-wide program that delivers a standard approach to the security assessment, authorization, and continuous monitoring for cloud products and services. Amazo
items.title: Amazon Aurora DSQL is now in scope for FedRAMP Moderate

items.summary: Amazon Cognito now supports importing users with password hashes in CSV user imports. Previously, users imported from a CSV file had to reset their passwords on first sign-in. Now, you can include password hashes in your CSV file so that imported users can sign in immediately with their existing credentials.  When creating a CSV import, you specify the password hashing algorithm used by your source system. Amazon Cognito imports these users and verifies their password against the imported hash o
items.title: Amazon Cognito now supports importing users with password hashes

items.summary: AWS Graviton4-based R8g database instances are now generally available for Amazon Aurora (MySQL and PostgreSQL compatibility) and Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL, MySQL, and MariaDB in Asia Pacific (Hyderabad, Melbourne, Malaysia), Europe (London, Paris, Zurich), AWS GovCloud (US-East), South America (Sao Paulo), and Mexico (Central) regions. Additionally, M8g instances are now supported for Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL, MySQL, and MariaDB in US West (N. California), Asia Pacific (Mumbai, Sydney, Hong Ko
items.title: Amazon RDS and Aurora now support R8g and M8g database instances in additional AWS Regions

items.summary: AWS Glue SAP OData connector and zero-ETL integrations are now available in AWS GovCloud (US-West) and AWS GovCloud (US-East) Regions, with support for Amazon DynamoDB, Salesforce, and SAP OData as sources. Customers operating in regulated environments can now replicate data from these sources into Amazon Redshift, Amazon S3, or other supported destinations without building or maintaining custom data pipelines.  The SAP OData connector allows you to extract data from SAP systems exposing OData s
items.title: AWS Glue SAP OData connector and zero-ETL integrations are now available in AWS GovCloud (US) regions

items.summary:
Amazon GameLift Streams now supports assigning an IAM role to a stream session, enabling your application to securely access resources in your AWS account, such as Amazon S3 buckets and DynamoDB tables. With this launch, you can pass a RoleArn parameter when starting a stream session, and your application automatically receives short-lived, auto-refreshing AWS credentials through the standard AWS SDK credential resolution chain — no application code changes required. 
Previously, customers who n
items.title: Amazon GameLift Streams now supports IAM role credentials for stream sessions

items.summary: Amazon OpenSearch Service now supports one-click migration from legacy OpenSearch Dashboards to OpenSearch UI, for both OpenSearch domains and serverless collections. OpenSearch UI is the new, zero-downtime, serverless interface for search and unified observability across multiple data sources. With this launch, the multiple tenants and thousands of saved objects you created in legacy OpenSearch Dashboards become reusable in your OpenSearch UI applications, reducing the operational complexity of
items.title: Amazon OpenSearch UI now supports one-click dashboard migration

items.summary:
Today, AWS Billing and Cost Management (BCM) announces support for Cost Efficiency widget in BCM Dashboards. You can now view cost efficiency trends alongside Cost Explorer, Budgets, and reports for Savings Plans and Reserved Instance coverage and utilization reports. This provides a unified view of your spending, commitments, and optimization performance in a single, tailored dashboard. 
The Cost Efficiency widget displays your efficiency score over time, showing how your efficiency across your
items.title: Track cost efficiency trends directly in Billing and Cost Management Dashboards with the new Cost Efficiency widget

items.summary:
Amazon EC2 now surfaces the AWS Systems Manager (SSM) Parameter Store parameters associated with public AMIs directly in the AMI metadata. When you describe a public AMI, the response includes the associated public SSM parameter, making it easy to discover and reference in your configurations. 
Previously, finding the SSM parameter associated with a public AMI required searching through SSM parameter namespaces manually. Now, when you describe a public AMI, the response includes the public
items.title: Amazon EC2 now surfaces the public SSM parameters associated with public AMIs

items.summary: Amazon S3 Event Notifications now include system-generated tags in events delivered to all destinations including Amazon EventBridge, Amazon SQS, Amazon SNS, and AWS Lambda. System-generated tags are metadata labels attached to your bucket by AWS services. You can use these tags to filter events from thousands of buckets with a single EventBridge rule, instead of listing each bucket name individually.  To get started, enable S3 Event Notifications on your general purpose buckets through the AWS
items.title: Amazon S3 Event Notifications now include system-generated tags

items.summary: Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL 19 Beta 2 is now available in the Amazon RDS Database Preview Environment, allowing you to evaluate the pre-release of PostgreSQL 19 on Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL. You can deploy PostgreSQL 19 Beta 2 in the Amazon RDS Database Preview Environment that has the benefits of a fully managed database.  PostgreSQL 19 introduces parallel autovacuum with configurable worker limits, so routine maintenance no longer bottlenecks large databases. The new REPACK CONCURRENTLY command
items.title: PostgreSQL 19 Beta 2 is now available in Amazon RDS Database Preview Environment

items.summary:
Amazon EC2 High Memory U7in-24TB instances (u7in-24tb.224xlarge) are now available in AWS Europe (Paris) region. U7i instances are part of the AWS 7th generation and are powered by custom fourth-generation Intel Xeon Scalable processors (Sapphire Rapids). U7in-24TB instances offer 24 TiB of DDR5 memory, enabling customers to scale transaction processing throughput in a fast-growing data environment. U7i instances offer up to 45% better price performance over existing U-1 instances. 
U7in-24
items.title: Amazon EC2 High Memory U7in-24TB instances now available in AWS Europe (Paris) region

items.summary: You can now transition objects to S3 Standard-Infrequent Access (S3 Standard-IA) and S3 One Zone-Infrequent Access (S3 One Zone-IA) as soon as the day they are created, without the previous 30-day minimum retention in S3 Standard. These storage classes offer up to 40% lower storage costs than S3 Standard while still providing millisecond access when needed, making them ideal for backups, log analytics, and compliance workloads where data becomes cold within hours or days.  To get started, create
items.title: Amazon S3 removes 30-day minimum for transitions to S3 Standard-IA and S3 One Zone-IA

items.summary:
Starting today, Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (Amazon EC2) G7e instances accelerated by NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs are now available in the AWS Europe (Frankfurt, Stockholm) and Asia Pacific (Mumbai) Regions. G7e instances offer up to 2.3x inference performance compared to G6e. 
Customers can use G7e instances to deploy large language models (LLMs), agentic AI models, multimodal generative AI models, and physical AI models. G7e instances offer the highest performance f
items.title: Amazon EC2 G7e instances now available in additional regions

items.summary: Amazon Relational Database Service (RDS) now supports R8gd database instances in 12 additional regions and and M8gd database instances in 6 additional Regions with Optimized Reads for Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL, RDS for PostgreSQL, RDS for MySQL, and RDS for MariaDB.  R8gd and M8gd instances deliver up to 165% better throughput and up to 120% better price-performance over R6g instances for Aurora PostgreSQL. Optimized Reads uses local NVMe-based SSD block storage to store ephemeral data such as te
items.title: Amazon RDS and Aurora expand R8gd and M8gd to additional Regions

items.summary:
Amazon RDS now allows up to four storage modifications per database instance within a rolling 24-hour window. These modifications let you increase the size, change the type, and adjust the performance of your RDS storage volumes. You can start a new modification right after storage optimization for the previous modification is complete without having to wait for the six-hour cool-off period to complete. 
This enhancement improves operational agility for scaling storage capacity or adjusting perf
items.title: Amazon RDS now supports up to four storage modifications in 24 hours

llm.detail: axios's shouldBypassProxy fails to normalize IPv4-mapped IPv6 addresses (e.g. ::ffff:127.0.0.1), so a host that should match a NO_PROXY entry is not recognized and the request is routed through the proxy anyway. This is a follow-up to the earlier CVE-2025-62718 fix, which did not cover this address form. An attacker who controls proxy configuration or can influence request targets can force traffic meant to bypass the proxy through it, enabling interception or SSRF-style routing.
llm.recommended_action: Upgrade axios to the patched version once released (check the GHSA advisory for the fixed range) and run `npm audit`; interim, avoid relying on NO_PROXY with IPv4-mapped IPv6 hosts and pin proxy settings server-side.
llm.summary: axios NO_PROXY bypass via IPv4-mapped IPv6 addresses; incomplete fix for CVE-2025-62718.
llm.why_it_matters: axios is a direct dependency in your npm stack and is commonly used for server-side HTTP calls in Next.js/React and Node services, where NO_PROXY often protects internal/metadata endpoints; a bypass can leak internal traffic through an untrusted proxy or defeat egress controls.

items.summary: axios's shouldBypassProxy does not recognize IPv4-mapped IPv6 addresses, allowing NO_PROXY bypass (incomplete fix for CVE-2025-62718)
items.title: GHSA-pjwm-pj3p-43mv: axios
